title: Existing DuskDS repository is read-only
fix: The existing-project lane needs a writable checkout because Dusk Forge and Cargo create build artifacts and may update ignored tool output. Keep the protected source unchanged; create a trusted writable clone or Git worktree, verify that its full commit matches the intended source, and enter that writable absolute path in Build.
safeNextStep: Do not weaken permissions on protected source just to make Studio pass. Use a writable clone or worktree, run the clean-tree and full-commit command before Prepare project, and confirm the same commit and clean unignored tree again after build and test.

title: Node or npm cannot prepare the DuskDS starter
fix: Install Node.js 24.18.0 or newer in the Node 24 LTS release line, open a new terminal, and verify node -p "process.versions.node". On Windows PowerShell, use npm.cmd --version and npx.cmd so a restricted script policy does not resolve the blocked npm.ps1 or npx.ps1 shim.
safeNextStep: Do not weaken PowerShell execution policy. Confirm the supported Node version and npm.cmd --version, then rerun the exact Prepare project command shown for Windows.

title: Wallet is on the wrong chain
fix: Studio does not connect to a wallet before launch. For future planning, chain ID 745 / 0x2e9 is the expected Testnet identity; wait for a reviewed activation notice before adding or switching a wallet network.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview and review the expected chain metadata. Do not add or switch a wallet network until the live workflow is activated.

title: No injected wallet found
fix: No wallet action is required in Studio before launch. When wallet support is activated, use a reviewed EIP-1193 wallet and verify the exact network request before connecting.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview. Wallet connection and EVM starter actions are not active in Studio yet.

title: Wallet request rejected
fix: The wallet declined the request. Retry only if you understand the requested network action.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview and review the planned wallet boundary before approving any future network request.

title: Insufficient DUSK for gas
fix: Funding is not active in Studio before launch. After activation, use only the reviewed official Testnet funding route and verify its current status before moving tokens.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview and review the planned Access stage. Funding is not active in Studio until an official route is reviewed and activated.

title: RPC unavailable or slow
fix: There is no DuskEVM RPC health check to retry in Studio before launch. If you independently test a published endpoint later, Failed to fetch can indicate RPC availability, firewall/proxy, DNS, TLS, or CORS behavior.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview. Studio does not run an EVM network check yet; use current official sources before treating any endpoint as live.

title: DuskDS public node check is unavailable or slow
fix: The live DuskDS read-only request did not complete. Check your network, proxy, DNS, and the public Testnet node status. If the browser reports a security-policy error, use the separate CSP recovery entry. Do not disable TLS or browser security.
safeNextStep: Return to DuskDS Access or Inspect and retry the same read-only check. If the public node is still unavailable, use the manual W3sper lane and keep the step blocked until a valid latest-block result is observed.

title: Blockscout unavailable
fix: A contract may still be deployed even if the explorer is lagging or unavailable.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview and review the planned Inspect stage. Studio does not treat the explorer as an active inspection surface yet.

title: Foundry is not installed
fix: Foundry is not required for the current pre-launch reference. It becomes relevant only after the reviewed local Build workflow is activated.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview and review the planned Build stage. Studio does not run EVM builds or deploy commands yet.

title: Bridge or funding step is pending
fix: Bridge timing can vary. Keep source and destination transaction details and avoid sending duplicate bridge attempts without checking status.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview. Funding is not an active Studio flow; use official Dusk support if this concerns an external transaction.

title: Contract verification failed
fix: Studio does not submit DuskEVM verification before launch. For future recovery, the compiler version, optimizer settings, constructor arguments, and exact contract path must match deployed bytecode.
safeNextStep: Return to the DuskEVM pre-launch overview and review the planned Inspect stage. Studio does not submit contract verification.

title: Browser blocks the public Dusk node check
fix: Start the exact current npm package shown on Studio's Local Studio page. It includes the reviewed public Testnet node origin in its browser security policy; do not reuse a stale local build.
safeNextStep: Do not weaken browser security or add CSP, certificate, or network exceptions. If the check still fails, confirm endpoint and network availability, then use the manual W3sper Access lane.

title: Forge found a Windows wasm-opt shim
symptoms: %1 is not a valid Win32 application
fix: On Windows, npm Binaryen can put an extensionless wasm-opt shim first on PATH. Install a native Binaryen wasm-opt.exe or remove that shim from PATH for Forge runs. Forge can skip optional optimization and still build contract plus data-driver WASM.
safeNextStep: Run DuskDS preflight. If only wasm-opt is warning, continue with dusk-forge build all or fix the optimizer later.

title: Forge build fails under a deep Windows path
fix: On Windows, native Rust build scripts can fail when a Forge project is nested under a deep path. Local Actions creates the starter beneath its managed DuskDS root and shows the exact created path after success. Use only a relative subfolder in Build, then re-run dusk-forge check and dusk-forge build all.
safeNextStep: Leave the optional Build subfolder blank, or restart Local Studio with DUSK_STUDIO_DUSKDS_PROJECT_ROOT set to a normal absolute short local folder before creating the starter.

title: Rust 1.94.0 WASM target or rust-src is missing
fix: The Studio pins generated Dusk Forge projects to Rust 1.94.0. Install that toolchain with the wasm32-unknown-unknown target and rust-src component before building native Dusk contracts.
safeNextStep: Run rustup toolchain install 1.94.0 --component rust-src --target wasm32-unknown-unknown, then re-run DuskDS preflight.

title: Forge build fails on newer Rust stable
fix: A WSL Ubuntu build failed with Rust stable 1.96.1 at the WASM link step, while the same Forge starter built and tested with Rust 1.94.0. Keep generated starters pinned to Rust 1.94.0 until Dusk Forge and its dependencies are re-verified on newer stable Rust.
safeNextStep: Install Rust 1.94.0 with rust-src and the wasm32 target, then confirm rust-toolchain.toml says channel = "1.94.0" before rebuilding.

title: Native data-driver WASM was not built
fix: Use dusk-forge build all so Forge builds both contract WASM and data-driver WASM from the same source.
safeNextStep: Use DuskDS Build and keep contract and data-driver outputs from the same source revision.

title: The deployed contract's data driver is unavailable
fix: A successful contract deployment does not prove that a data driver is available. Read /on/contract:<id>/metadata and require driver_available: true before using /on/driver:<id>/... schema, encode, or decode helpers.
safeNextStep: If driver_available is false, do not mark post-deploy driver checks complete. Uploading the driver requires a signed owner action outside Studio; follow the current official workflow rather than inventing an upload command.

title: Rusk Wallet is missing for native deployment
fix: Native Dusk contract deployment uses a manual Rusk Wallet path outside Studio. Install and review wallet commands before signing.
safeNextStep: Do not automate signing until wallet setup, fee estimation, and deploy policy are reviewed.

title: Make is missing for optional shortcuts
fix: Make is useful for template shortcuts, but the Studio uses Dusk Forge CLI directly. Use dusk-forge check and dusk-forge build all when Make is unavailable.
safeNextStep: Run DuskDS preflight and continue if all required Forge and Rust checks pass.

title: Optional WASM packaging tools are missing
fix: wasm-pack and wasm-tools are useful for advanced packaging and inspection, but the basic Forge build does not require them. Treat wasm-opt as optional optimization.
safeNextStep: Run dusk-forge check and build the unchanged starter before adding optional packaging steps.

title: Data driver does not match the contract WASM
fix: Rebuild contract WASM and data-driver WASM from the same source revision and confirm exported functions, events, and schema expectations match.
safeNextStep: Use DuskDS Build to rebuild both artifacts before connecting frontend or indexer code.

title: DUSK and LUX units are mixed up
fix: Native integration docs use LUX for base units. Convert deliberately: 1 DUSK equals 1,000,000,000 LUX.
safeNextStep: Keep unit conversion near the call site and add tests around any displayed amount or transaction amount.

title: Transaction is executed but not finalized
fix: Do not treat inclusion or execution as the end of the lifecycle. Wait for finality before considering the transaction irreversible.
safeNextStep: Use DuskDS Inspect and the transaction lifecycle docs before reporting success to users.

title: Rusk Wallet is pointed at the wrong node
fix: Align Rusk Wallet state, prover, and optional archiver URLs with the node you are testing against. Use CLI overrides or a wallet config file deliberately.
safeNextStep: Check wallet settings before contract-call or contract-deploy, and keep signing manual in your trusted terminal.

title: DuskEVM mempool assumption is wrong
fix: DuskEVM runtime behavior differs from generic public-mempool assumptions. Avoid product logic that depends on observing a public pending transaction pool.
safeNextStep: Inspect confirmed transactions through Blockscout and design app states around submitted, confirmed, and failed outcomes.

title: dusk-forge test needs a Linux environment
symptoms: no distribution with the supplied name
fix: Contract and data-driver builds can pass on Windows, but the reviewed VM test needs Linux. Run wsl -l -v first. If Ubuntu-24.04 is missing, use wsl --install -d Ubuntu-24.04 and complete any Windows-requested reboot and first launch before installing the reviewed DuskDS tools.
safeNextStep: Return to Setup, open Conditional and optional tools, run the WSL with Ubuntu 24.04 install commands, then repeat that exact check. You may keep Windows build artifacts, but do not record VM-test evidence until the Linux check and test pass.

title: Foundry build passes but signature cache warning appears
fix: Foundry can pass build/test while failing to write its local signature cache. Create or unlock %USERPROFILE%\.foundry\cache, run from a normal terminal, or ignore the warning when the compile/test result already passed.
safeNextStep: Do not rerun deploys because of this warning alone. Confirm whether forge build and forge test passed, then fix cache permissions later.

title: The official template and Studio Forge starter use different project shapes
fix: The current official Smart Contracts on DuskDS guide names my-first-contract as the canonical starting point and uses Make-based commands. Studio's separately pinned and tested Dusk Forge CLI creates a different project shape. Both target DuskDS and produce contract plus data-driver WASM, but their commands and artifact paths are not interchangeable.
safeNextStep: Choose one project shape: follow the official Make commands inside my-first-contract, or follow Studio's dusk-forge commands inside a Studio Forge starter. Do not mix commands or artifact paths between them.

title: Hedger is mentioned but not ready for Studio automation
fix: Dusk docs mention Hedger as the route toward confidential EVM flows, but the audited public docs and repositories do not yet give enough implementation detail for a guided template or deploy flow.
safeNextStep: Build and inspect the public DuskEVM path first; do not create Hedger templates until source-backed implementation docs are available.

title: Citadel flow is being treated as production-ready too early
fix: Citadel 2 is source-backed and important, but it is a specialized identity/access protocol with security and policy boundaries. Treat it as experimental/review-required before using it for compliance or user access decisions.
safeNextStep: Read the Citadel protocol docs, repository README, specs, and threat model before designing a production flow.

title: Dusk Connect cannot find a wallet provider
fix: Dusk Connect discovers compatible wallet providers. If no provider is announced, the first-party wallet extension may be missing, locked, disabled for the site, or on the wrong network.
safeNextStep: Install or unlock the Dusk Wallet Extension, then test provider discovery before building transaction UI.

title: Wallet integration is being mixed with node integration
fix: Dusk Connect is for wallet discovery and wallet/provider requests. W3sper and node HTTP/GraphQL/RUES APIs are for network reads, transaction lifecycle, contract calls, and infrastructure behavior.
safeNextStep: Use Dusk Connect for account access and W3sper for node access, then join them only at the app-flow layer.

title: Archive history query is sent to a non-archive node
fix: Historical Moonlight transfer queries such as moonlightHistory and fullMoonlightHistory require an archive-enabled node or public archive endpoint.
safeNextStep: Use an archive endpoint for historical deposit/accounting workflows and keep realtime event handling separate.

title: Moonlight and Phoenix addresses are being mixed up
fix: Moonlight is the public account model used for exchange-style deposits and withdrawals. Phoenix is shielded and incompatible with Moonlight-only transfer expectations.
safeNextStep: Check the encoding reference and choose one address model deliberately before accepting deposits or displaying addresses.

title: Verifiable build cannot run because Docker or the build image is missing
fix: Verifiable builds are a production/audit gate, not the first local build. They require Docker and the matching locked Dusk verifiable build image.
safeNextStep: First pass dusk-forge check, build all, and tests; then add verifiable builds as a release gate with the documented image.

title: Unstable token example is being copied into production
fix: The transparent-token and electronic-money-token repositories describe useful patterns but explicitly warn that function signatures are unstable and the projects are in development.
safeNextStep: Study them as examples, then design and review a production contract separately with tests, audits, and verifiable builds.

title: DUSK decimals are wrong for an integration
fix: Native DUSK uses 9 decimals. ERC20/BEP20 environments commonly use 18 decimals, so migrations, exchange systems, and displays must convert deliberately.
safeNextStep: Add unit conversion tests around every balance, deposit, withdrawal, and migration display.

title: Contract call arguments exceed the wallet/provider limit
fix: The wallet provider API documents a bounded contract-call argument size. Reduce or split call arguments instead of bypassing wallet/provider checks.
safeNextStep: Check dusk_getCapabilities, inspect maxFnArgsBytes, and redesign large payloads before asking users to sign.

title: DRC20 or DRC721 watchAsset verification failed
fix: The Dusk Wallet provider verifies token metadata and DRC721 ownership before persisting watched assets. A failed watchAsset request can mean the contract ID, token ID, metadata, current ownership, or data-driver expectations do not match.
safeNextStep: Read the contract with the canonical data driver and verify metadata/ownership before prompting the user again.

title: Stake Abstraction is calling stake_from_contract directly
fix: Contracts cannot call stake_from_contract directly. The official Stake Abstraction flow triggers it through a Transfer Contract contract_to_contract transfer into the Stake Contract.
safeNextStep: Follow the Stake Abstraction guide sequence: move funds into the contract, call contract_to_contract with fn_name stake_from_contract, then implement receive_unstake and receive_reward callbacks.

title: Rusk Wallet secrets are being routed through the Studio
fix: The public Studio must not request, store, transmit, or log wallet secrets. Rusk Wallet signing, deploy, and staking actions stay manual unless a reviewed loopback-only policy is approved.
safeNextStep: Run Rusk Wallet commands in your own terminal and use Studio only for checklists, docs, and local companion scaffolding.

title: Citadel session cookie is treated like harmless metadata
fix: Citadel session cookies are bearer credentials in the protocol flow. They should be handled as sensitive access material, not logged, pasted into public tools, or stored by the Studio.
safeNextStep: Review the Citadel specs and security docs before building any service-provider flow. Keep Citadel in docs-only mode until security review is complete.

title: Native standards are being presented as audited production templates
fix: The contracts standards source is useful and important, but the Studio should present it as evolving source-backed building blocks, not as audited production templates.
safeNextStep: Use the standards as implementation reference, then add VM tests, data-driver tests, verifiable builds, and audit review for production use.

title: Hyperlane is assumed to be a ready Dusk app path
fix: A public dusk-network Hyperlane fork exists, but the audit did not find a Dusk-specific implementation guide suitable for a Studio flow.
safeNextStep: Treat Hyperlane as source context until Dusk publishes integration docs, deployed addresses, chain configs, and safety guidance.

title: T-REX or OnchainID is being positioned as an official DuskEVM starter
fix: The Dusk-network forks expose useful regulated Solidity patterns, but the audit did not find a Dusk-specific guided deployment path or production recommendation.
safeNextStep: Keep these as advanced source-context links and build the normal DuskEVM deploy/verify loop first.
